message.content:
Steel kissed skin before the prisoner realized the blade had moved.

At first it was only a pressure along his ribs, a cool line drawing itself beneath the grime and sweat of his shirt. Then the pressure deepened, and the coolness turned to warmth, and then to the bright, animal sting of sliced flesh.

He jerked against the chains bolted into the stone, their iron bite digging deeper into his wrists. The sound of it—skin parting, the faint wet rasp—seemed louder than his own strangled breath.

“Listen,” the demon murmured at his ear. Its voice was soft, almost affectionate. “Don’t waste this.”

It stood close enough that he could feel the weight of it, smell the faint reek of old smoke and something coppery and sweet that wasn’t blood but remembered it. Its lips never touched his skin, but its words brushed him like another blade.

The knife moved again.

This time he felt every nerve it woke. The point traced along the shallow groove it had already made, then drifted lower, opening a second line a finger’s breadth away. The cuts were not deep; that was the cruelty. Just far enough to part the skin, to let blood well up and cling before gravity pulled it in slow, irregular rivulets down his side. He could feel each track, warm and sticky, mapping his own body for him.

“You think the worst is the pain,” it whispered. “You’re used to that idea. It’s comforting, isn’t it? Pain has limits. Pain ends.”

Hot breath feathered against the shell of his ear as the knife paused, resting its blunt spine against his side. The demon’s other hand flattened on his chest, just over his heart, as if feeling the tremor of it. Its fingers were too long, the joints wrong: not claws, not quite, but something that remembered claws.

“You’re hoping you’ll faint soon.” The demon sounded amused, almost bored. “That your mind will tear itself away and hide somewhere. But you won’t. Not yet. I won’t allow it.”

A third cut, this one angled to cross the first two, slow and deliberate. The blade sank just a little deeper this time. There was a brief, grating drag as it met the faint resistance of muscle, then slid past. His breath hitched, broke into a choking half-scream that died against the gag knotted between his teeth.

He tasted cloth and rust and his own blood.

“Mm. There it is.” The demon’s tone warmed, as though savoring a good wine. “Not the sound. The moment under it. The thought.”

Its hand pressed a fraction harder over his heart. “That little flare of Oh. This is real. This is happening. It will go on.”

Blood crawled down his abdomen now in thin lines, soaking into the waistband of his torn trousers. It wasn’t pouring—no gushing fountains, no cinematic spray. Just steady, patient seepage. The stone under his bare feet had gone slick where it dripped, making his legs tremble more with every shift of weight.

He tried to twist away when the knife rose again, but there was nowhere to go. The chains had been fixed so that he could move just enough to feel hope, never enough to escape. Metal bit into raw skin. Warmth swelled around his wrists where he had already struggled them bloody.

“You’ve been imagining this since they dragged you down here,” it whispered. “Haven’t you? Little scenes in your mind. Will they break my fingers? Will they burn me? Will they peel me open?”

The blade touched the inside of his arm, where the skin was thin and sensitive. He flinched, a full-body shudder.

“How frightened you were of your own imagination,” it went on, voice low and steady. “All those little pictures. So proud of how terrible they were. Terrible.” The word curled with contempt. “You have no idea how small your horrors are.”

The knife began to move, a slow, careful line from the crook of his elbow toward his wrist. The demon wasn’t trying for tendons or veins. It stayed shallow, skimming the topmost layer of flesh with excruciating precision. Each millimeter felt like its own eternity: steel moving, skin parting, the delayed flare of new pain adding to the chorus of the old.

He watched, because he couldn’t not. The cut gleamed for a heartbeat, bright and wet, then the blood rose in tiny beads before swelling together and spilling over. It wasn’t much, barely enough to drip, but seeing himself opened like that—seeing the inside of him on the outside—made his stomach heave.

“You see?” the demon crooned. “You are not a mystery. You are not
